title: The Two Dimensional Dynamical Bulk Boundary Correspondence: Beyond Two Band Models
abstract: A dynamical equivalent of the bulk-boundary correspondence has been suggested to occur in one and two dimensional topological models following sudden quenches. Depending on the topological invariant of the time evolving and initial phases involved large boundary contributions to a dynamical free energy occur. Moreover they occur periodically between the critical times at which this dynamical free energy becomes non-analytic, \emph{i.e.}~at dynamical quantum phase transitions. At these critical times the eigenvalue spectrum of the Loschmidt matrix which underlies the dynamical free energy closes its gap. The boundary contributions are understood to be due to zero-modes or in-gap bands of this matrix, forming a close analogy with equilibrium topological models and their edge modes. The exact cause of this phenomena and its generality remain unknown. In this article we test the dynamical bulk-boundary correspondence for a complicated two dimensional topological superconductor with a rich phase diagram, allowing quenches between many different Chern numbers. We show that there is no straightforward correspondence between the equilibrium phases quenched between and the dynamical bulk boundary correspondence. Furthermore the correspondence can depend on the orientation of the edges, suggesting a possible weak topological variant.
summary: This paper investigates the dynamical bulk-boundary correspondence in 2D topological superconductors after sudden quenches. Using the Loschmidt amplitude as a diagnostic tool, the authors tested this concept across various topological phase transitions. They conclude that the dynamic correspondence is not simply dictated by the initial and final equilibrium Chern numbers, suggesting a more nuanced, edge-dependent topological structure.
main_problem: The research investigates a dynamical equivalent of the bulk-boundary correspondence (DBBC) for topological models subjected to sudden quenches, extending beyond simple two-band descriptions.
main_result: The authors find that there is no straightforward correlation between the equilibrium phases quenched between and the observed dynamical bulk-boundary correspondence. Furthermore, this correspondence can depend on the specific orientation of the edges, suggesting a weak topological variant.
method: The analysis relies on calculating the Loschmidt amplitude/return rate ($\ell(t)$) and identifying Dynamical Quantum Phase Transitions (DQPTs) where these quantities become non-analytic or exhibit zero eigenvalues in the Loschmidt matrix spectrum.
model_or_system:
The primary model studied is a complicated two-dimensional topological superconductor defined on a honeycomb lattice, allowing quenches between states characterized by different Chern numbers ($
u$).
key_observables:
Loschmidt amplitude/return rate ($\ell(t)$), Loschmidt eigenvalues ($\lambda_{n,k}(t)$), and the system's Chern number ($
u$).
important_parameters:
The quench path between initial and final topological phases (e.g., $
u: 0 	o 1$ or $
u: -2 	o -3$), and the geometry of the edges (armchair vs. zig-zag).
main_assumptions: The analysis is performed in a cylindrical geometry, which may limit direct applicability to physical systems with single open edges; the exact cause and generality of the phenomenon remain unknown.
figures_summary: Figures plot the lowest Loschmidt eigenvalue $\lambda_{0,k}(t)$ vs. momentum/time for various quenches on different edge types (AC/ZZ), illustrating where in-gap modes appear near DQPTs; Figure 6 shows the return rate derivative ($\dot{l}(t)$) across quench paths.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the theoretical framework of DBBC, applies it to a complex 2D topological superconductor model undergoing quenches between different Chern number sectors, and concludes by detailing specific results showing deviations from simple equilibrium predictions based on edge orientation.
why_interesting: This work bridges non-equilibrium quantum dynamics (Loschmidt echo) with topological invariants (Chern numbers), providing a complex, quantitative test case for extending bulk-boundary principles into time evolution.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The topological phase diagram for the topological superconductor [104], Eq. (7), with α = 0.4J and ∆= 0.4J. White circles are the points in the phase diagram between which we quench, see table I.

FIG. 2. In panels (a,b) the lowest Loschmidt eigenvalue λ0,k(t) as a function of momentum and time is plotted. This is calculated for a system of size 102 × 100 unit cells in the open and periodic directions respectively for both zig-zag (ZZ, panel a) and armchair (AC, panel b) edges. Panel (c) shows a cut at a particular time for the zig-zag edges with the lowest 12 eigenvalues λn,k(t) and the lowest bulk eigenvalue calcu- lated for periodic boundary conditions (N = 2002) giving the limit of the shaded region. In (a,b) the bulk critical regions appear as vertically orientated curved bright lines, and the lowest eigenvalue of the band of eigenvalues crossing the gap appear as horizontally...

FIG. 3. In panels (a,b) the lowest Loschmidt eigenvalue λ0,k(t) as a function of momentum and time is plotted for both zig-zag (a) and armchair (b) edges. Panel (c) shows a cut at a particular time for the zig-zag edges with the lowest 12 eigenvalues λn,k(t) and the lowest bulk eigenvalue. See Fig. 2 and table I for more details. See the main text for a discussion of the results.

FIG. 4. In panels (a,b) the lowest Loschmidt eigenvalue λ0,k(t) as a function of momentum and time is plotted for both zig-zag (a) and armchair (b) edges. Panel (c) shows a cut at a particular time for the armchair edges with the low- est 12 eigenvalues λn,k(t) and the lowest bulk eigenvalue. See Fig. 2 and table I for more details. See the main text for a discussion of the results.

FIG. 5. Bandstructure examples for the model, (7), where α = 0.4J and ∆= 0.4J and further parameters are as in table I. The topologically protected bands can be seen crossing the gap, Chern numbers ν are as marked on the figures. Note as this is a chiral system left moving and right moving bands are on opposite edges of the system.
